name: Plaster Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Plaster. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for plaster surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength optimized for plaster absorption and minimal substrate damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for balanced cleaning efficiency and resolution

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence level sufficient for contaminant removal while preserving plaster substrate

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal with controlled thermal penetration

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning without excessive dwell time

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning coverage and efficiency

power.description: Typical range for industrial laser cleaning of delicate materials like plaster to avoid thermal damage; starting at 100 W for light contamination removal. Max safe below 500 W to prevent substrate cracking due to plaster's low thermal conductivity (~0.5 W/m·K). Source: Manufacturer specs from IPG Photonics YLR series and studies on laser cleaning of gypsum-based plasters (e.g., J. Cult. Herit. 2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective range for 1064 nm cleaning on plaster; slower speeds (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (2000-3000 mm/s) for light. Recommended 1500 mm/s balances efficiency and safety. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and equipment manuals (e.g., Adapt Laser Systems).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of plaster; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond reduces HAZ but less common at 1064 nm for industrial use. 20 ns minimizes heat accumulation on low-melting-point plaster (~150°C dehydration). Source: Laser physics research in Opt. Lasers Eng. (2015) on ablation of calcium sulfate materials.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed 1064 nm lasers in cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for plaster to achieve fast coverage without excessive heat buildup, trading off speed vs. thermal load. Higher rates (>50 kHz) risk accumulation on low-conductivity substrates. Source: Academic literature, e.g., Appl. Surf. Sci. (2019) on stone/plaster cleaning parameters.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal without ablating plaster; damage threshold ~1.5-2.0 J/cm² for gypsum due to reflectivity (~0.3 at 1064 nm) and low melting point. Recommended 0.8 J/cm² for safe surface preparation. Source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Appl. Phys. (2017) and historical artifact cleaning papers.

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for precision vs. area coverage; 300 μm recommended for plaster to focus energy on contaminants while sparing porous substrate. Smaller for fine details, larger for broad industrial prep. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaners.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers on plaster to gradually remove without damage. Recommended 2 passes for uniform results in industrial settings. Source: Industry case studies, e.g., restoration projects in Constr. Build. Mater. (2020).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage without gaps or overexposure; 50% recommended for plaster cleaning to optimize energy distribution and minimize uneven heating. Typical 30-80% based on scan patterns. Source: Process optimization studies in Lasers Manuf. Mater. Process. (2016).
